coredata.dc:title: Study on the mechanism of stimulated emission of carbon dots: Influence of concentration aggregation

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xin
Wang, Boyang
Ma, Fengying
Cheng, Liwen
Zhang, Yongqiang
Hu, Yongsheng
Lu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of highly efficient, environmentally friendly, wavelength-diverse luminescent materials, exhibiting concentration-dependent emission properties. Understanding the mechanism of concentration-induced aggregation effects on their excited-state emission characteristics is crucial for developing high-performance laser devices based on CDs. In this study, we systematically investigate the spontaneous and stimulated emission actions of CDs with varying degrees of concentration-induced aggregation. Our findings reveal that the degree of aggregation does not inherently alter the type of charge carrier recombination, which remains dominated by excitonic recombination. However, with increasing aggregation, exciton–exciton annihilation becomes more prominent, reducing both total radiative efficiency and radiative decay rates. This ultimately results in an increased threshold for the stimulated emission. The findings in this work may advance the understanding of the excited-state emission mechanisms in CDs and provide valuable guidance for the design of CD-based laser materials and device architectures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Concentration aggregation
Stimulated emission
Time-resolved photophysical
Exciton–exciton annihilation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spectroscopic characterizations of each sample. (a) Photographs of CDs under daylight and UV light. (b) UV–visible absorption spectra, (c) PL spectra under 532 nm pumping, and (d) fluorescence lifetime and PLQY of CDs.

(a) PL spectra and (b) TRPL decay of C0. (c) Dependence of PL integrals under CW excitation versus the pump power densities. The solid lines are linear fits on a log–log scale, and the slope is 1.01, 0.98, 0.99, 0.99, and 0.94 for C0, C1, C5, C10, and C20, respectively. (d) Plots of PL0 versus the pump fluence. The solid lines are linear fits on a log–log scale, in which the slope is 0.95, 0.90, 1.12, 0.95, and 1.02 for C0, C1, C5, C10, and C20, respectively.
Calculated (a) radiative efficiencies and (b) decay rates at different exciton densities for C0, C1, C5, C10, and C20.

Characterization of the CDs lasers. (a) Emission spectra of C0 laser under different pump fluences. (b) High-resolution spectra of C0 laser, the inset is the photograph of the far-field spot. (c) Emission spectral peak intensity and FWHM change with pump fluences. (d) Summarized threshold for different CDs lasers.
Rate coefficients obtained by globally fitting TRPL decay curves for CDs.

head.ce:title: Study on the mechanism of stimulated emission of carbon dots: Influence of concentration aggregation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Laboratory of Zhongyuan Light, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Laboratory of Zhongyuan Light

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Physical Science and Technology, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of highly efficient, environmentally friendly, wavelength-diverse luminescent materials, exhibiting concentration-dependent emission properties. Understanding the mechanism of concentration-induced aggregation effects on their excited-state emission characteristics is crucial for developing high-performance laser devices based on CDs. In this study, we systematically investigate the spontaneous and stimulated emission actions of CDs with varying degrees of concentration-induced aggregation. Our findings reveal that the degree of aggregation does not inherently alter the type of charge carrier recombination, which remains dominated by excitonic recombination. However, with increasing aggregation, exciton–exciton annihilation becomes more prominent, reducing both total radiative efficiency and radiative decay rates. This ultimately results in an increased threshold for the stimulated emission. The findings in this work may advance the understanding of the excited-state emission mechanisms in CDs and provide valuable guidance for the design of CD-based laser materials and device architectures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have shown important application prospects in biomedical imaging [1], optical sensing [2,3] and information anti-counterfeiting [4] due to their high quantum yield (QY), good photostability, wide source of precursors and biocompatibility [5,6]. As one of the important parts of optoelectronic device applications, laser devices based on CDs have made great development in the past few years, not only the emission wavelengths successfully cover from visible [7] to near-infrared region [8], but also show a wide range of application potential in fields such as laser imaging [9], holographic displays [9], vortex beams [10] and solid-state illumination [11].

CDs usually exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent and concentration-dependent luminescence properties [12]. Having weak or no excitation wavelength-dependent properties is an important prerequisite for achieving excited emission from CDs [13], which is mainly affected by the degree of concentration of the density of electronic states in the excited state, while concentration dependence is manifested by the fact that the emission wavelengths and photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of CDs vary with the degree of aggregation of CDs. Aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) phenomena associated with the concentration of CDs have been widely reported [14,15]. While the concentration dependence of the luminescence wavelength facilitates the tuning of the CDs laser wavelength, the ensuing prevalent ACQ is not conducive to obtaining a high optical gain for CDs, which in turn prevents CDs from achieving lasing, especially in the solid state [16]. So far, few studies have revealed the relationship between the degree of CDs aggregation and its excited radiation mechanism and lasing properties.

The origin of the photoluminescence (PL) of CDs is rather complicated and is generally considered to include quantum confined effect, surface state emission, carbon core, and molecular state luminescence [17–20]. From the point of view of excited state carrier dynamics, it is still poorly understood whether the PL of CDs originates from free carriers or exciton radiative recombination. Recent studies of CDs light-emitting diodes have shown that CDs electroluminescence may originate from exciton radiation recombination, including the first excited singlet exciton [21] and hot excitons [22], at the same time, different excited-state radiation processes, such as phosphorescence [23], thermally activated delayed fluorescence [24] and long persistent luminescence [25] have also been observed in CDs, and these findings play an important role in the understanding of luminescence mechanisms in CDs. Further investigation of excited radiation mechanism of CDs under higher excitation intensities, especially in different aggregation states, should be conducive to a more in-depth understanding of the luminescence mechanism, which would provide important guidance for the design of CDs laser materials and device structures.

In this study, we systematically explore the influence of concentration-induced aggregation on the kinetic processes of excited-state carriers in CDs and establish a correlation between the extent of aggregation and the lasing threshold properties of CDs. The results demonstrate that the PL in CDs primarily originated from exciton radiative recombination, irrespective of the aggregation level. However, as the degree of aggregation decreases, the rate of monomolecular radiative recombination increases, enhancing the efficiency of exciton radiative recombination at high intensities and contributing to a lower threshold for stimulated emission in CDs. The above results not only reveal the concentration aggregation-dependent carrier recombination mechanism of CDs but also highlight the importance of tuning the stimulated emission properties by controlling the aggregation state of the CDs.

The CDs studied in this work were synthesized according to the literature [9], and a stock solution of CDs was obtained at a concentration of 25 mg/mL (denoted as C0). The degree of aggregation of CDs was controlled by dilution using the solvent hydrazine hydrate, and 1, 5, 10, 20-fold dilutions of the solutions were obtained, denoted as C1, C5, C10, and C20, respectively. Fig. 1 a gives the photographs of the samples under both daylight and UV light, which shows that with the increase of the degree of aggregation, the color of the CDs solution under daylight irradiation varies from light to dark, and those under UV light show a homogeneous deep red color. Fig. 1b shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of each sample, showing two distinct absorption regions near 330 nm and 570 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* leaps of the carbon nuclei and the n-π* leaps of the surface states associated with the surface C = O/C = N bonds, respectively [26]. As the degree of aggregation of CDs solution increased, the absorbance gradually increased, and the peak position remained unchanged, which indicated that the luminescence center of CDs does not change with the increase in concentration. Fig. 1c further gives the PL spectra of each sample, all of which showed a main peak accompanied by a weak shoulder peak. It can be observed that the CDs luminescence peaks significantly redshifted with the increase of concentration from 610 nm to 645 nm, and the spectral linewidth was in the range of 70 to 80 nm. This is a typical phenomenon of redshift due to an increase in the aggregation state caused by the energy transfer [27]. Fig. 1d gives the fluorescence lifetimes of different CDs (lifetime decay curves and fitting see Fig. S1), and the aggregation state has almost no effect on the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs. This indicates that the fluorescence lifetimes reflect more of the intrinsic state emission related to the internal structure of CDs, which is not much affected by the degree of molecular aggregation [28]. However, the CDs solution exhibits significant ACQ properties [29]. This phenomenon is a common problem in materials such as colloidal quantum dots, perovskites, and organic semiconductors [30]. The reason for the ACQ can be understood as follows: as the concentration increases, the distance between the luminescent molecules is brought closer together, and when the distance between the molecules is close enough, processes such as intermolecular interactions and Förster resonance energy transfer occur, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. CDs are no exception, and their particular polymer shell structure affects the surface functional groups and their electronic environment during aggregation, further causing quenching [31]. As seen from the PLQY variation in Fig. 1d, which gradually decreases from 26.6 % for C20 to 10 % for C0.

To investigate the origin of the radiative recombination of CDs, we first studied the PL spectra of each sample under 532 nm continuous-wave (CW) excitation. The results for the C0 sample and the other samples are shown in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S2, respectively. Further, we examined the time-resolved PL (TRPL) decay of each sample under pulsed laser pumping (532 nm, 700 ps, 1 kHz) as shown in Fig. 2b and Fig. S3. Fig. 2c summarises the relationship between the PL integral intensity and the excitation optical power density for each sample. It can be observed that the slopes of CDs solutions with different degrees of aggregation (in log–log scale) are close to 1, indicating that the density of absorbed photons is proportional to the density of emitted photons, which is the typical monomolecular (exciton) carrier recombination luminescence (see Supplementary Note 1). [32] Fig. 2d gives the maximum instantaneous emission rate (maximum PL intensity) PL0, the relationship between the radiative recombination and pump fluence [33]. The dependence of PL0 on the excitation energy also reflects the different radiation recombination channels (see Supplementary Note 2). PL0 also increases linearly with increasing the pump fluence, with all the slopes near 1. The above results clearly show that the carrier recombination mechanism of CDs is an exciton channel and is independent of the degree of aggregation.

Based on the above analyses, we further adopt the following differential rate equation to describe the dynamics (see Supplementary Note 1) [34]:
(1)
-
d n
dt
= A n + B
n 2
where n is the excitation density, t is the time, A and B are the excitonic emission and exciton–exciton annihilation (EEA) rate coefficients, respectively. Eq. (1) yields the numerical expression for TRPL [31].
(2)
PL
( t )
∼
dn
dt
h ν
= A n
t

We use Eq. (2) to globally fit the PL decay under different pumping fluences, as shown in Fig. S4, and the fitting results are in good agreement with the experimental data. The obtained coefficients A and B are given in Table 1 . The coefficient A gradually increases as the concentration of CDs decreases, while the coefficient B gradually decreases, suggesting that as the degree of CDs aggregation is weakened, the exciton radiation is enhanced in the range of excitation intensities examined, while the EEA process is concurrently weakened. This change favors the exciton radiation efficiency, which is consistent with the change in PLQY.

To visualize the effect of monomolecular and EEA rate coefficients more intuitively, we further calculated the exciton radiative recombination efficiencies (Fig. 3 a) as well as the total carrier decay rates (Fig. 3b) for different exciton concentrations. The radiative efficiencies were calculated by the ratios of the radiative rates to the total decay rates (assuming no monomolecular loss for exciton emission). In Fig. 3a, for example, C0 is dominated by the exciton emission at low exciton densities (n ≤ 1016 cm−3), and its radiation efficiency is close to 1. As the exciton density increases, the exciton annihilation process leads to an increase in losses and a gradual decrease in the radiation efficiency, increasing to n ≥ 1019 cm−3 and decreasing to 0. The reason for this is that an increase in the density of exciton leads to an increase in exciton–exciton interactions, which ultimately leads to a decrease in radiation efficiency. [35] The results for other samples are also consistent with this rule. It is worth noting that the radiation efficiency gradually shifts to the right as the degree of aggregation of CDs decreases, indicating that the lower the degree of aggregation, the higher the radiation efficiency under the same excited-state density, which is in line with the change of PLQY.

Fig. 3b further gives the variation process of exciton emission and EEA in CDs. For all samples, the exciton emission dominates at low excitation density, and the EEA rate gradually becomes higher than the exciton emission rate with the increase of excitation density. The carriers tend to have different recombination channels in different excited-state density regions. As the aggregation degree of CDs decreases, the coordinates of the intersection point of the An term and the Bn2 term gradually shifted rightward from (2.23 × 1017 cm−3, 4.06 × 1026 cm-3s−1) to (6.95 × 1018 cm−3, 1.93 × 1028 cm-3s−1), and the carrier decay rate gradually increases. This indicates that the exciton emission can dominate at higher excitation densities, which is favorable for CDs to obtain higher optical gain.

To further establish a link between the aggregation state and the stimulated emission properties, we characterized the laser properties of each CDs sample. A 532 nm pulsed laser (7 ns, 10 Hz) was used to pump the CDs sample (Fig. S5). The CDs laser was supported by the Fabry-Perot resonator effect provided by the cuvette [36] as well as multiple scatterings of the CDs sample. [9] Fig. 4 a gives the evolution of the spectrum of the C0 sample with excitation intensity. The C0 sample shows a significant spectral narrowing from > 50 nm to < 10 nm. As can be seen from its high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 4b), the spectral linewidth after lasing is only 0.11 nm and is accompanied by a bright far-field spot (inset of Fig. 4b).

Fig. 4c gives the relationship of spectral intensity and linewidth with the pump intensity for the C0 sample. A clear intensity threshold and spectral narrowing can be seen. Except for C20, the other three samples, C1, C5, and C10, show the same phenomenon as shown in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7. These results clearly show the appearance of random lasers for the C0, C1, C5, and C10 samples. No laser emission was observed for the C20 samples in the experiment, which was mainly caused by the fact that most of the pump light could not be effectively absorbed when the solution was diluted higher than 10 times [17]. Fig. 4d summarizes the lasing thresholds of the C0, C1, C5, and C10 samples, which are 15.0, 13.1, 12.7, and 10.2 mJ/cm2, respectively. The decreasing trend agrees well with the results predicted by the exciton dynamics. This suggests that when constructing the CDs lasers, especially solid-state CDs lasers, a rational adjustment of the degree of CDs aggregation is crucial, and the widely adopted host–guest doping might become an effective strategy beyond that of pure CDs as the gain material.

In conclusion, we systematically investigate the spontaneous and stimulated emission properties of CDs across varying aggregation states. We found a significant impact of the aggregation on the carrier recombination kinetic processes of CDs. The results indicates that while the carrier recombination channel is predominantly governed by exciton across different aggregation levels, increased aggregation markedly influences the exciton dynamics. The EEA process becomes more pronounced with greater aggregation, leading to a reduction in both radiative efficiency and emission rates, which ultimately results in an elevated lasing threshold. The findings in this work not only provide an in-depth understanding of the luminescence mechanism of CDs but also offer guidance for the development of high-performance CD gain materials and laser devices.

Materials: Citric acid (CA) and L-cysteine (L-Cys) were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Co. Ethanol, formamide, and polyethylene glycol were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. All chemicals were analytically pure.

Synthesis: 1 g CA and 0.6299 g L-Cys were dissolved in deionized water (15 mL). The solution was sonicated for 5 min to form a homogeneous solution. The solution was then transferred to a household microwave oven and heated at 700 W for 3 min. At the end of the reaction, solid blue CDs were obtained. Blue CDs powder was dissolved in 6 mL of deionized water and transferred to a 25 mL PTFE-lined autoclave, and then 4 mL of formamide was added to form a homogeneous solution, which was heated at 180℃ for 8 h. At the end of the reaction, the cooled reaction solution was dialyzed in a 500 Da dialysis bag for two days, and the dialysate was lyophilized to obtain the CDs powder used in this paper.

Characterization: Ultraviolet–visible spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-3101 PC spectrophotometer. PL was measured by fiber optic spectrometer (Ocean Optics, QE Pro) under the excitation with either the CW 532 nm laser (Cobolt, Samba™ 100) or a Q-Smart Nd: YAG laser. The absolute PLQYs were measured on an Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrofluorometer. Time-resolved spectroscopic measurements were performed using an AQNL-6000 laser. Transient PL emissions were measured by using a photodetector and oscilloscope (SIGLENTSDS2352X-E). For laser characterization measurements, the Q-Smart Nd: YAG laser was used as the pump. The laser emission spectra and their high-resolution spectra were recorded using an Ocean Optics, QE Pro fiber optic spectrometer, and a HORIBA iHR 550 spectrometer, respectively. The pumping fluence was acquired by a Newport 2936C laser power meter.

Xin Li: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Boyang Wang: Resources, Conceptualization. Fengying Ma: Software, Investigation. Liwen Cheng: Visualization. Yongqiang Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yongsheng Hu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Siyu Lu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Henan (242300421699).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optlastec.2024.112296.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
